title: Praxis Special Education: Teaching Students with Behavioral Disorders and Emotional Disturbances (5372) - 200 Source Questions + 300 Guided Review
description: Contains 200 source diagnostic questions and 300 source-derived guided-review tasks; the assistant audit found 128 distinct source content kernels. Two 100-item diagnostics, an exact-blueprint 120-item simulation, detailed feedback, and native learning support for K-12 EBD specialists.
contentReview: Assistant audit completed: the 200 source questions contain 128 distinct content kernels and 72 parallel variants under the normalized answer-set/rationale/reference test. The 300 additional activities are assistant-reviewed guided reasoning transformations, not independent exam questions. The 500-distinct-question target is not met.
simulationLabel: 120-question Behavioral Disorders and Emotional Disturbances simulation
disclaimer: Independent preparation material, not affiliated with or endorsed by ETS. Results are not official forms, scaled scores, pass predictions, licenses, diagnoses, eligibility, placement, FBA or BIP determinations, mental-health or crisis services, medical or legal advice, or substitutes for individualized assessment and current requirements.

domains.label: Development and Characteristics of Students with EBD
Planning and Managing the Learning Environments

items.prompt: When interpreting behavioral or emotional development, a kindergarten teacher notices that one child reaches several language milestones later than classmates. What is the most appropriate first interpretation?
choices: Development follows broad patterns, but individual variation requires information from multiple sources and settings before conclusions are drawn.
Any difference from a class average proves a disability.
The child should immediately receive a fixed disability label.
Chronological age no longer matters when planning instruction.
items.rationale: Milestones provide useful reference points, not stand-alone diagnoses. Educators should document functioning across domains, contexts, and time and involve qualified team members before attributing a pattern to disability. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: When interpreting behavioral or emotional development, a student solves multistep problems during small-group instruction but rarely begins the same work during a noisy whole-class period. What should the teacher examine first?
choices: Assume the student has forgotten the underlying skill.
Compare task demands, environmental conditions, supports, and performance across the two contexts.
Lower every academic expectation for the student.
Treat the difference as deliberate noncompliance.
items.rationale: Performance reflects an interaction among learner skills, task demands, supports, and context. Comparing conditions can identify a removable barrier before the team changes expectations or infers a stable trait. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: When interpreting behavioral or emotional development, a teacher wants to understand a learner’s self-regulation needs. Which information is most useful?
choices: A single global rating completed months ago.
The individual support profile without current observations.
Observations of triggers, strategies used, recovery, and successful conditions across routines.
The average behavior of the rest of the class.
items.rationale: Self-regulation is best understood through specific, current observations of what precedes difficulty, how the learner responds, which strategies help, and whether patterns differ by setting or task. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: When interpreting behavioral or emotional development, a multilingual learner has difficulty following rapid oral directions in English but demonstrates the task after a peer models it. Which interpretation is best?
choices: The performance automatically establishes an intellectual disability.
Visual modeling should be removed to reveal the true score.
Home-language information is irrelevant to educational planning.
Language access may be affecting the demonstration of knowledge, so the team should gather culturally and linguistically appropriate evidence.
items.rationale: Difference is not disability. The team should distinguish language acquisition and access from disability by using multiple methods, appropriate language supports, and information about performance in the learner’s languages. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: When interpreting behavioral or emotional development, a middle school student with behavioral or emotional support needs is embarrassed when an adult uses age-inappropriate materials for a functional routine. What principle should guide revision?
choices: Use age-respectful materials while matching the instructional steps and supports to the learner’s current needs.
Match all materials to developmental level regardless of age.
Stop teaching functional skills until grade-level mastery is reached.
Ask peers to complete the routine for the student.
items.rationale: Instruction can be individualized without sacrificing dignity. Materials and contexts should be chronological-age respectful, while prompts, task steps, and response modes are adapted to the learner’s present performance. items.prompt: When interpreting behavioral or emotional development, a student performs a social routine only with one familiar adult. Which plan best promotes generalization?
choices: Repeat the skill only in the original setting until it is perfect.
Practice the same skill with planned variations in people, materials, and natural settings, then fade prompts.
Introduce several unrelated target skills at the same time.
Wait for generalization to occur without programming it.
items.rationale: Generalization is more likely when instruction deliberately varies relevant people, materials, and settings, reinforces the skill in natural routines, and gradually transfers stimulus control away from adult prompts. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: When interpreting behavioral or emotional development, a team is writing a learner profile. Which statement is most useful for planning?
choices: The student is described only by a global support-needs label in most situations.
The student has autism and therefore prefers routines.
The student follows a two-step visual routine independently and needs one verbal prompt when the sequence changes.
The student is developmentally much younger than peers.
items.rationale: Objective descriptions identify a demonstrated strength, a specific condition, and the support needed. Global labels and assumptions based on category do not tell the team what to teach or how to measure change. items.prompt: When interpreting behavioral or emotional development, a learner’s participation drops after a recent family move and change in transportation. How should the school respond?
choices: Attribute the change entirely to the individual support profile.
Remove the student from general education immediately.
Ignore nonacademic factors because they are outside instruction.
Consider environmental changes alongside disability-related needs and coordinate supports without blaming the learner or family.
items.rationale: Development and learning are influenced by environmental as well as biological factors. A responsive team gathers context, protects privacy, stabilizes access, and avoids treating a situational change as proof of a new disability need. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: When interpreting behavioral or emotional development, a teacher records one difficult morning and says a student has lost a previously learned skill. What evidence is needed before changing the program?
choices: Collect repeated observations and work samples under representative conditions and compare them with prior data.
Make the change because the most recent event is always most valid.
Use the disability label as the only source of evidence.
Wait until the annual review and gather no additional data.
items.rationale: A single observation may be affected by health, context, fatigue, or task features. Repeated measures and comparison with baseline information support a more reliable decision about whether performance truly changed. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: A student with emotional or behavioral support needs reads grade-level text accurately but avoids multi-paragraph writing when the task is unstructured. Which response is best?
choices: Provide identical remediation in every academic area.
Teach a visible planning routine, model paragraph organization, offer bounded choices, reinforce task engagement, and monitor both writing and behavior data.
Remove writing tasks whenever avoidance occurs.
Have an adult permanently complete the planning and writing.
items.rationale: The response should address the documented writing demand and the conditions associated with avoidance. Explicit organization support, predictable choices, reinforcement, and progress data preserve the grade-level goal while teaching independent engagement.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. The evidence identifies a task-specific need, not an identical deficit in every subject. The response should address the documented writing demand and the conditions associated with avoidance. Explicit organization support, predictable choices, reinforcement, and progress data preserve the grade-level goal while teaching independent engagement.

Correct. The response should address the documented writing demand and the conditions associated with avoidance. Explicit organization support, predictable choices, reinforcement, and progress data preserve the grade-level goal while teaching independent engagement.

Not the best answer. Removing the task prevents instruction in the needed skill. The response should address the documented writing demand and the conditions associated with avoidance. Explicit organization support, predictable choices, reinforcement, and progress data preserve the grade-level goal while teaching independent engagement.

Not the best answer. Permanent adult completion reduces access to instruction and independence. The response should address the documented writing demand and the conditions associated with avoidance. Explicit organization support, predictable choices, reinforcement, and progress data preserve the grade-level goal while teaching independent engagement.

items.prompt: For a student with behavioral or emotional support needs, a student with autism covers their ears during fire-drill announcements and then misses directions. Which support is most responsive?
choices: Interpret the response as intentional defiance.
Exclude the student from every future drill.
Plan a predictable, accessible warning and communication routine while teaching the student how to use it.
Require exposure without preparation until the response stops.
items.rationale: Sensory and communication needs can affect access and behavior. A predictable warning, accessible direction, and taught coping or communication response support participation while preserving safety requirements. items.prompt: For a student with behavioral or emotional support needs, a student with ADHD begins work when directions are chunked and a checklist is visible. What does this pattern suggest?
choices: The student knew the work and was choosing to fail before.
The checklist has changed the academic standard.
The disability affects intelligence rather than self-management.
Executive-function supports are improving task initiation, organization, and monitoring.
items.rationale: ADHD may affect executive functions such as initiation, working memory, inhibition, and organization. External structures can support performance without changing the learning target or implying weak ability. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: Elena correctly requests a break during role-play in the support room, but she pushes materials away when a lab report is assigned in science. Which interpretation and response are most appropriate?
choices: Treat the science behavior as willful noncompliance because Elena has already demonstrated the skill elsewhere.
Repeat the definition of a break request only in the support room until Elena can recite it without error.
Examine the cues and consequences in science, then practice the request there with a shared prompt and reinforce it before gradually fading support.
Remove lab reports from Elena's schedule because the skill has not generalized independently.
items.rationale: Demonstrating a response in role-play does not show that the relevant classroom cues control it under authentic demand. Assessing the science context and programming in-setting practice, reinforcement, and fading addresses a likely performance or generalization gap.
choiceRationales: Prior demonstration does not prove deliberate refusal under different cues and task conditions.
More decontextualized recitation does not transfer stimulus control to science.
This tests contextual barriers and deliberately teaches use where the breakdown occurs.
Eliminating the task reduces access to instruction and does not build generalization.

items.prompt: A point system initially increased Sam's on-time return from breaks, but the effect has weakened and Sam says the available rewards are no longer worth earning. What should the team do next?
choices: Double every point requirement immediately so the system becomes more challenging.
Keep the system unchanged for the semester because changing rewards makes data invalid.
Remove all earned points following one late return to restore the consequence's importance.
Reassess current preferences with Sam, test several feasible outcomes, and adjust the menu while continuing to measure on-time returns.
items.rationale: The effectiveness of a reinforcer can change with satiation, development, and context. Updating the menu through student input and observed behavioral effects preserves the target contingency while allowing the team to verify renewed effectiveness.
choiceRationales: Increasing response effort can further weaken an already ineffective contingency.
Stable procedures are not useful when data and student report show loss of value.
Response cost does not establish what currently reinforces timely return.
Current preference and outcome data support an individualized, testable adjustment.

items.prompt: Direct observation shows that when independent reading passages exceed Cole's instructional level, he puts his head down; the teacher then sends him to a computer with easier text. Which statement is the most defensible functional hypothesis?
choices: When independent text is too difficult, Cole puts his head down, which is followed by removal of that text; the behavior may be maintained by escape from an excessive reading demand.
Cole avoids reading because he has a negative attitude and needs to value education more.
Cole puts his head down because putting one's head down is an avoidant behavior.
Whenever Cole is tired, sensory stimulation from the computer causes the behavior in every class.
items.rationale: A useful hypothesis identifies an observable antecedent, behavior, and typical consequence and cautiously proposes a function supported by those data. It can then guide tests involving task level, requests for help, and consequences without treating inference as fact.
choiceRationales: It links observed task difficulty and task removal to a testable escape hypothesis.
An inferred attitude is not operational and is not established by the observations.
This circular statement merely renames the behavior rather than explaining its outcome.
The observations do not establish fatigue, sensory reinforcement, or occurrence across all classes.

items.prompt: A teacher asks the behavior specialist to send a student's detailed incident log to every staff member in the building ‘so everyone knows what to expect.’ What is the most appropriate response?
choices: Send the full log because all school employees automatically need access to every student's behavior information.
Identify staff with a legitimate educational role, share only the information needed to implement safety and instruction, and use objective language through approved channels.
Refuse to discuss any behavior support with classroom staff because all educational records are confidential.
Remove the student's name but include enough unusual details that staff can infer the student's identity.
items.rationale: Effective collaboration must be balanced with privacy: relevant staff need actionable information for their educational responsibilities, not unrestricted access to a detailed history. Minimum-necessary, objective communication also reduces stigma and rumor.
choiceRationales: Employment at the school does not by itself establish a need for the complete record.
This limits disclosure to role-relevant, implementation-focused information through proper channels.
Confidentiality permits appropriate sharing with personnel who need information to serve the student.
Indirect identification can still disclose protected information and is not a valid safeguard.

items.prompt: A graph shows no improvement after two weeks of a new antecedent support, but fidelity checks show that the planned pre-correction occurred in fewer than half of observed opportunities. What conclusion should the team draw?
choices: The student's disability is too severe for antecedent interventions to work.
The support has been implemented adequately because it appeared at least once in each week.
The outcome is not yet a fair test of the plan; the team should address implementation barriers, coach staff, and gather stable fidelity and behavior data before judging effects.
The functional hypothesis must be wrong because behavior did not improve immediately.
items.rationale: Low implementation fidelity prevents the team from attributing unchanged behavior to the intervention itself. Improving feasibility and staff performance, then monitoring both fidelity and outcomes, creates interpretable evidence for a later decision.
choiceRationales: Student severity cannot be inferred from a plan that was seldom delivered.
Occasional use does not meet the planned opportunity-based implementation criterion.
This separates an implementation failure from a possible intervention failure.
An unchanged short series under low fidelity does not disconfirm the functional hypothesis.

items.prompt: Peers have begun teasing Amina because her teacher announces her daily behavior points and posts her chart beside the classroom door. Amina's replacement-skill use is also declining. What revision is most appropriate?
choices: Continue public posting because embarrassment may motivate Amina to earn more points.
Ask classmates to comment on Amina's chart so the class shares responsibility for her improvement.
End all feedback and reinforcement so no behavioral information is visible.
Move individualized feedback and records to a private format, retain reinforcement for the taught skill, and teach classroom expectations through universal supports that do not identify Amina.
items.rationale: Individual behavior data and feedback should protect dignity and avoid making the student a public target. Private delivery preserves an evidence-based contingency, while universal instruction supports the classroom without disclosing Amina's status.
choiceRationales: Public embarrassment is stigmatizing and may make the intervention aversive.
Peer review further exposes private information and can intensify social harm.
Privacy can be restored without abandoning instruction and effective reinforcement.
This retains the functional support while separating it from public identification.

items.prompt: During unfamiliar multistep work, Omar tears materials after repeated errors; the teacher then completes the hardest step for him. Which replacement response should be taught first if the team confirms an assistance function?
choices: A brief help request that produces a prompt for the difficult step, followed by reinforcement and gradual fading as Omar becomes more independent.
A promise to avoid tearing materials for the entire grading period.
An apology written after the teacher has completed the assignment.
Silent waiting until the teacher happens to notice that Omar is struggling.
items.rationale: A concise help request can obtain the same needed assistance more safely and efficiently than tearing materials. Prompting, honoring, and reinforcing the request establishes it, while systematic fading prevents support from replacing skill development.
choiceRationales: It directly accesses assistance and includes a plan to build later independence.
A distant promise does not provide a usable response at the point of difficulty.
An apology addresses aftermath but does not replace the assistance-seeking behavior.
Waiting is inefficient and does not reliably contact the needed outcome.

items.prompt: A family supports their child's regulation but cannot use the school's hourly point card during evening routines. The school team wants continuity without imposing an unworkable plan. What should the specialist do?
choices: Tell the family that fidelity requires using the school card exactly as written at home.
Ask about family priorities and routines, co-design a feasible shared cue or communication method, and review data together while allowing setting-specific procedures.
Stop sharing progress information because home and school interventions are different.
Ask the student to enforce the school's procedure with family members each evening.
items.rationale: Authentic family collaboration centers family knowledge, priorities, and feasibility rather than demanding identical procedures across settings. A common goal or cue can support coherence while home and school use contextually appropriate methods.
choiceRationales: Rigid replication disregards family context and is not necessary for shared outcomes.
This creates meaningful continuity through collaborative, feasible, data-informed planning.
Different procedures do not prevent useful two-way communication about progress.
The child should not be made responsible for directing adult implementation.

items.prompt: When selecting or administering an assessment for a student with EBD, what does a criterion-referenced interpretation show?
choices: Performance relative to specified skills or standards rather than relative standing among peers.
The student’s percentile rank in a norm group.

items.rationale: Criterion-referenced results describe mastery of defined content or performance criteria. Norm-referenced results describe relative position within a comparison group; neither alone establishes disability cause. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: During independent composition, Maya tears her worksheet and asks to visit the nurse only when prompts require organizing several ideas. Staff usually send her into the hallway. The team hypothesizes that the behavior helps her escape complex writing. What should the specialist do next to test that hypothesis before selecting an intervention?

items.rationale: Systematic direct observation can confirm whether difficult writing reliably precedes the behavior and whether leaving the task reliably follows it. Comparing demands and settings tests the escape hypothesis while avoiding an intervention based only on assumption.

Peer opinions may inform context but cannot establish a functional relation.
Visibility does not show that adult attention is the maintaining consequence.

items.prompt: A ninth grader enters first period agitated after an unpredictable bus ride and often argues when several adults give directions at the doorway. Which preventive support best addresses the pattern while preserving access to instruction?

Establish one predictable greeter, a brief visual arrival routine, and a practiced choice of two regulation activities before academic directions begin.

Routine exclusion can strengthen avoidance and reduces instructional access.
Multiple simultaneous reminders recreate the condition associated with escalation.

items.prompt: In a crowded locker area, Devon swears and shoves past peers. Observation shows that peers move away immediately, giving Devon space. Which replacement skill is most functionally equivalent to the behavior?

The request is efficient, socially appropriate, and matched to the identified escape-from-crowding function.
A later apology repairs harm but does not replace the behavior when space is needed.

items.rationale: Health supports in school must follow the individualized plan, staff training, delegation rules, and emergency procedures. Improvised treatment can create risk and exceed an educator’s role. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

"Create a new response based on what seems convenient." misses the observable definition, prevention, function, replacement skill, dignity, evidence, safety, or legal requirement. items.prompt: When coordinating a safe support without exceeding educator scope, a team wants to measure growth in practical daily functioning. Which target is most appropriate?
choices: The student will improve independence soon.
The student will earn higher intelligence-test scores.
The student will behave like same-age peers at all times.
Given a visual checklist, the student will complete four steps for purchasing a cafeteria item in four of five opportunities.
items.rationale: Adaptive goals should identify a meaningful conceptual, social, or practical routine and specify observable conditions, performance, and criteria. Vague comparisons do not support instruction or progress monitoring. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

items.prompt: A recently arrived multilingual student is described as defiant because she rarely makes eye contact and sometimes waits silently after whole-group questions. Before treating these actions as evidence of an emotional or behavioral disorder, what should the team do?
choices: Use only an English behavior rating scale because the concern occurs in an English-language classroom.
Work with a qualified interpreter and family, examine cultural and language expectations, and observe the student across people, tasks, and settings.
Assign daily consequences for nonresponse so the team can see whether the behavior is intentional.
Delay all inquiry until the student speaks English fluently enough to explain the behavior independently.
items.rationale: Culturally and linguistically responsive assessment tests alternative explanations and uses multiple sources rather than equating difference with disability. Cross-setting observation and family input help determine whether the concern is pervasive and educationally significant.
choiceRationales: A single English-only rating can confound language, culture, and disability.
Multiple culturally responsive sources reduce bias and clarify the meaning of the observed behavior.
Punishment before assessment assumes willful misconduct and may distort later observations.
Support and valid inquiry should not be postponed until English proficiency changes.

items.prompt: Nia can name her early signs of frustration but does not notice them during independent mathematics until she has already crumpled her work. Which instructional approach is most likely to build accurate self-monitoring?
choices: Give Nia a blank behavior chart and tell her to complete it whenever she remembers.
Ask the teacher to record incidents privately without discussing the observations with Nia.
Model the cueing routine, practice with brief task samples, teach Nia to record a specific early sign, compare her record with adult observations, and reinforce accurate use.
Have Nia write an essay explaining why emotional control matters before returning to mathematics.
items.rationale: Self-monitoring is a skill that requires explicit modeling, guided practice, a clearly defined response, feedback on accuracy, and reinforcement in the setting where it is needed. Gradual practice connects recognition of early signs to timely action.
choiceRationales: An unprompted blank chart provides neither discrimination training nor accuracy feedback.
Adult-only data can monitor behavior but do not teach Nia to observe her own cues.
The sequence directly teaches, checks, and strengthens accurate self-observation during relevant work.
An essay assesses verbal knowledge rather than in-the-moment self-monitoring performance.

items.prompt: When Luis refuses a difficult task, one teacher negotiates, another sends him out, and a third repeats the direction until he yells. The team wants to implement one behavior support plan across classes. What is the strongest first collaborative step?
choices: Email staff the name of the intervention and allow each teacher to interpret it for the class context.
Assign responsibility to the special educator so general educators do not need to alter their responses.
Try a different consequence in each class and keep whichever one produces the quietest week.
Agree on observable triggers and responses, define the prompt and consequence sequence, rehearse it with staff, and collect brief fidelity data in each class.
items.rationale: A shared operational plan and rehearsal reduce contradictory consequences that may maintain refusal, while fidelity data show whether the agreed support actually occurred. Consistent implementation is necessary before comparing student outcomes across classrooms.
choiceRationales: A label without procedures is unlikely to produce consistent staff behavior.
The plan must operate where the student encounters demands, not only with one specialist.
Uncoordinated consequences prevent meaningful interpretation and can reinforce refusal unpredictably.
Specific procedures, practice, and fidelity checks create a coherent cross-class implementation system.

items.prompt: During a sudden escalation, a student begins throwing heavy classroom materials toward occupied desks. What should the trained teacher do first?
choices: Move others from danger, summon designated assistance, use brief calm directions and the established crisis plan, and postpone problem solving until regulation returns.
Ask the student to explain the feelings and events that led to the escalation before anyone moves.
Attempt a physical restraint alone so other adults do not witness the incident.
Invite classmates to remind the student how the behavior affects the classroom community.
items.rationale: The immediate priority is reducing imminent risk through the school's established, trained response and the least intrusive safe actions available. Processing causes or repairing relationships belongs after the student and others are safe and regulated.
choiceRationales: This sequence protects people, obtains trained support, and separates crisis response from later teaching.
Demanding an explanation during acute escalation increases language load and delays protection.
Solo restraint creates serious risk and may violate training and policy requirements.
Peer involvement exposes classmates to danger and can intensify the event.

items.prompt: Specific praise no longer increases Andre's use of a coping card, and staff report that the same reward has been used for months. What is the best next step?
choices: Remove all positive reinforcement so Andre learns that coping is an expected responsibility.
Conduct a brief, age-respectful preference assessment, offer meaningful choices, and use data to evaluate whether selected consequences increase coping-card use.
Select a larger reward without asking Andre or observing whether it changes behavior.
Provide food after every class regardless of whether Andre uses the coping card.
items.rationale: Reinforcers are defined by their effect, and preferences can change over time. A current choice-based assessment followed by outcome data identifies consequences that are acceptable and actually strengthen the target replacement response.
choiceRationales: Removing reinforcement does not address why the replacement response has weakened.
Current preferences and behavior data allow the team to verify a consequence's reinforcing effect.
Size alone does not establish value and excludes the student's perspective.
Noncontingent delivery cannot selectively strengthen coping-card use and may be inappropriate.

items.prompt: An eighth grader with a successful behavior plan will attend a large high school where schedules, staff, and break procedures differ. Which transition action is most likely to maintain the student's success?
choices: Send the existing plan to the records office and assume receiving staff will implement it unchanged.
Wait for a serious incident at the high school before deciding which supports are necessary.

Include the student in planning, summarize triggers and effective replacement skills with data, arrange warm handoffs and practice in the new setting, and identify who will monitor early implementation.
Remove the behavior plan before promotion so the student begins high school without a label.
items.rationale: Transition planning should transfer effective knowledge while adapting supports to the new environment and strengthening the student's self-advocacy. Warm handoffs, practice, and early monitoring reduce gaps that a file transfer alone cannot address.
choiceRationales: Records do not ensure shared understanding, contextual adaptation, or implementation.
Waiting for failure sacrifices prevention and may allow avoidable disruption.
This coordinates people, settings, student participation, and data before the change occurs.
Ending effective support solely to avoid stigma disregards demonstrated need.

items.prompt: A student currently calls out during teacher-led discussion and is learning to request a turn. Which annual goal is written so progress can be measured meaningfully?
choices: The student will demonstrate better self-control and show respect during class.
The student will understand why interrupting is inappropriate in all school settings.
The student will improve participation enough to satisfy classroom teachers by the end of the year.
Given the class discussion cue, the student will use the agreed turn-request response in at least four of five observed opportunities across three consecutive weekly probes.
items.rationale: The selected goal specifies the context, observable replacement response, performance criterion, and repeated measurement schedule. Those features allow the team to collect comparable evidence and determine whether instruction is producing durable change.
choiceRationales: Terms such as better and respect do not define an observable performance standard.
Understanding is not directly measured and does not specify the desired classroom action.
Teacher satisfaction is subjective and lacks an explicit criterion or data method.
The cue, response, criterion, and repeated probes make progress observable and replicable.

items.prompt: Jalen bolts from the cafeteria on some days but remains throughout lunch on others. Staff disagree about whether noise, peer contact, or delayed food service is most relevant. What should the behavior specialist recommend first?
choices: Collect direct ABC and contextual observations across multiple lunch periods, including days without bolting, before refining the functional hypothesis.
Choose noise as the cause because cafeterias are generally loud and immediately issue headphones.
Move Jalen to eat alone indefinitely so none of the possible triggers can occur.
Average his grades from days with and without bolting to identify the cause.
items.rationale: Sampling both occurrence and nonoccurrence days allows the team to compare competing antecedent conditions and the consequences of leaving. Direct contextual data are needed before deciding which environmental change or replacement skill fits the pattern.
choiceRationales: Contrasting relevant lunch conditions can distinguish among the team's plausible hypotheses.
A common cafeteria feature is not evidence that noise controls this student's behavior.
Indefinite isolation is restrictive and removes opportunities without identifying function.
Academic grades do not measure the immediate variables surrounding cafeteria elopement.

items.prompt: Priya refuses long independent assignments but completes the same skills in short teacher-led segments. Which antecedent strategy is most appropriate while the team teaches sustained engagement?
choices: Permanently reduce Priya's curriculum to easier work so refusal is unlikely.
Divide the assignment into visible sections, let Priya choose the order of equivalent sections, signal brief completion points, and prompt the taught break request.
Allow Priya to end every assignment as soon as refusal begins so escalation is avoided.
Present the full assignment without warning and add missed work to the next day's task.
items.rationale: Chunking, bounded choice, predictable endpoints, and a functional break request reduce the response effort that precedes refusal while preserving the learning target. The team can then build endurance gradually from successful engagement data.
choiceRationales: Permanent simplification changes instructional expectations rather than scaffolding access to them.
The strategy modifies relevant antecedents and embeds an appropriate way to request relief.
Ending work contingent on refusal can strengthen refusal as an escape response.
Surprise and accumulated work increase the same demand conditions linked to refusal.

items.prompt: Malik disrupts a lunchtime game by grabbing equipment, and observation suggests he is trying to gain entry to the peer activity. Which instructional plan best matches that function?
choices: Give a weekly lecture about friendship and wait for Malik to apply the ideas independently.
Seat Malik at a separate table so peers cannot react to the grabbing.
Teach and rehearse a brief joining request, prompt it near naturally occurring games, arrange peer responses, and reinforce successful participation.
Require Malik to copy the rule against grabbing after every lunch period.
items.rationale: The plan teaches an efficient socially appropriate response that can access the same outcome—participation with peers—and practices it where the opportunity occurs. Coordinated peer responses make the replacement more reliable than grabbing.
choiceRationales: General information without situated practice is unlikely to replace the entry behavior.
Isolation removes the desired social opportunity instead of teaching access to it.
The taught request and arranged peer response directly match the peer-access function.
Rule copying is delayed and does not produce a usable way to join an activity.

items.prompt: Rosa independently uses a grounding routine in the resource room but not in science or physical education. What should the team do to promote generalization?
choices: Continue practice only in the resource room until Rosa uses the routine perfectly there.
Assume Rosa will transfer the routine once she is sufficiently motivated in other classes.
Replace the routine with a different coping skill in every setting so each class has its own method.
Teach common cues across settings, rehearse during authentic low-stress moments with relevant staff, reinforce use, and probe performance as prompts are faded.
items.rationale: Generalization is programmed by connecting the skill to cues, people, and conditions beyond the original teaching setting. Practice in authentic contexts, coordinated reinforcement, and planned prompt fading show whether the routine becomes portable and independent.
choiceRationales: Mastery in one room does not by itself establish transfer to other contexts.
Motivation is an assumption, not a plan for stimulus generalization.
Multiple unrelated routines increase learning demands and prevent use of a shared cue.
This deliberately extends stimulus control and checks independent use in target settings.

items.prompt: A fire alarm reminds Tessa of a frightening event; afterward she hides under a desk and may strike at anyone who approaches closely. Which school response is most trauma-sensitive and educationally useful?
choices: Coordinate a predictable post-alarm safety routine, offer physical space and limited choices, cue a practiced regulation response, and invite rather than require later discussion.
Arrange unannounced alarm exposure when the class is empty so Tessa becomes accustomed to the sound.
Question Tessa about the frightening event immediately after every alarm until she provides details.
Excuse Tessa from all academic expectations for the remainder of any day with an alarm.
items.rationale: Predictability, choice, distance, and a practiced regulation response reduce threat without forcing disclosure or broadly removing instruction. The team can support recovery and reentry while coordinating any further care through appropriate channels.
choiceRationales: The response increases felt safety and control while preserving a path back to learning.
Unplanned exposure can intensify distress and should not be improvised by school staff.
Demanding details during dysregulation can be intrusive and escalate the threat response.
Automatic full-day exclusion may reinforce avoidance and exceeds the support needed for recovery.

items.prompt: A student with an IEP receives a disciplinary removal that may constitute a change of placement. What action should the school-based team take as it determines the appropriate next steps?
choices: Apply the same extended removal used for students without disabilities and review the IEP after the student returns.
Convene the required manifestation review with relevant members, examine evaluation and observational information and the IEP, and determine both disability relationship and implementation issues.
Ask the classroom teacher alone whether the conduct seemed intentional and use that judgment as the legal decision.
Automatically conclude that every behavior of a student with an emotional disability is a manifestation.
items.rationale: A manifestation determination is an individualized team review of relevant information, including whether the conduct had the required relationship to disability and whether the IEP was implemented. Neither disability status nor perceived intent alone resolves the question.
choiceRationales: A possible change of placement triggers disability-specific procedural protections before routine exclusion proceeds.
The required team and evidence review address both statutory manifestation questions.
One person's intent judgment is neither the required membership nor the required analysis.
Eligibility does not make every act a manifestation; the prescribed relationship must be evaluated.

items.prompt: A universal behavior screener indicates elevated internalizing risk for a quiet sixth grader whose grades have recently declined. What is the appropriate interpretation and next step?
choices: Diagnose an emotional disability from the screening score and begin special education immediately.
Ignore the result because the student has not disrupted class or received discipline referrals.
Treat the score as one risk indicator, review multiple data sources, provide timely support, and follow evaluation and consent procedures if a disability evaluation is warranted.
Tell teachers the student has an anxiety disorder so they can lower all expectations.
items.rationale: Screening identifies possible risk; it does not diagnose a condition or establish special education eligibility. Combining the result with observations, student and family information, academic data, and appropriate procedures supports an equitable response.
choiceRationales: A screening measure alone cannot establish diagnosis, eligibility, or service need.
Internalizing concerns may be educationally important even without disruptive behavior.
This uses screening for early response while preserving a valid multi-source evaluation process.
Assigning an unestablished diagnosis breaches appropriate interpretation and may reduce access to instruction.

items.prompt: During group work, Isaiah begins pacing, clenching his hands, and answering in short phrases but is still responsive to the teacher. Which response best fits this early escalation phase?
choices: Deliver a detailed lecture about consequences while the whole group listens.
Stand close, block Isaiah's movement, and insist on immediate eye contact.
Continue increasing task demands so Isaiah does not learn that agitation changes expectations.
Reduce verbal load, increase physical space, offer two safe options, and cue the regulation strategy practiced when Isaiah was calm.
items.rationale: Early signs create an opportunity for low-arousal de-escalation before safety deteriorates. Brief language, space, bounded choice, and a familiar coping cue reduce demands while helping the student regain control without an audience or power struggle.
choiceRationales: Lengthy public correction adds language, attention, and potential shame during escalation.
Crowding and forced eye contact can increase perceived threat and agitation.
Escalating demands ignores observable warning signs and may accelerate the crisis cycle.
The response matches the phase and prompts an already learned regulation pathway.

items.prompt: A student earns time with a favorite drawing app for beginning work, but frequent access has reduced its effect. The team wants the reinforcement plan to remain useful over time. Which adjustment is best?
choices: Offer a rotating menu based on current preferences, pair tangible outcomes with specific social feedback, and thin the schedule gradually only when performance data remain strong.
Make the app available throughout every class regardless of work initiation so the student never loses interest.
Replace reinforcement with increasingly severe penalties whenever work does not begin.
Keep the same app and schedule indefinitely because consistent plans should never respond to satiation.
items.rationale: Varying effective options reduces satiation, pairing them with naturally available feedback supports maintenance, and data-based thinning avoids withdrawing reinforcement faster than the skill can sustain. The consequence still remains contingent on the target response.
choiceRationales: This addresses changing value while building a practical path toward natural reinforcement.
Noncontingent continuous access may further reduce value and cannot strengthen initiation selectively.
Punishment does not solve reinforcer satiation or teach reliable work initiation.
Consistency means implementing a sound decision rule, not ignoring evidence of lost effectiveness.

items.prompt: In the first days of a plan for escape-maintained refusal, yelling increases. Staff also report that they sometimes cancel the task after yelling because the taught break request takes too long. What should the specialist recommend?
choices: Add a harsher consequence immediately because any early increase proves the plan is too lenient.

Check safety and implementation, make the break request fast and well prompted, adjust the task for successful engagement, and prevent yelling from inconsistently producing task cancellation when it is safe to do so.
Return to canceling every task after yelling because the student's initial response shows the demand is inappropriate.
Ignore all escalation, including safety warning signs, until the behavior eventually decreases.
items.rationale: An early increase does not identify one cause, especially when staff responses are inconsistent. Fidelity review, an efficient alternative, appropriate task support, and safe consequence consistency address both implementation problems and the confirmed escape function.
choiceRationales: Severity does not correct a slow replacement response or inconsistent escape consequence.
This repairs the functional plan while preserving safety and attainable instruction.
Reliable cancellation after yelling can strengthen the very response the plan targets.
Safety monitoring remains essential; extinction is never a reason to disregard risk.

items.prompt: A student who wants peer interaction hovers near cooperative groups, makes insulting jokes, and is then excluded. Which peer-mediated support is most appropriate?
choices: Tell peers to tolerate every joke so the student always gains group access.
Ask one peer to supervise and correct the student's behavior throughout the school day.
With willing participants, structure cooperative roles, teach the student an entry and comment routine, prepare peers to respond naturally, and monitor participation and social validity.
Move the student to independent work whenever group activities are scheduled.
items.rationale: Structured roles and explicit entry skills create successful, reciprocal opportunities for the desired peer contact. Preparing willing peers and monitoring both outcomes and acceptability avoids turning classmates into enforcers or requiring them to accept harmful behavior.
choiceRationales: Peers should not be required to accept insults as the price of inclusion.
A classmate should not be assigned an adult-like surveillance role.
This teaches reciprocal access in authentic activities while protecting voluntary peer participation.
Routine isolation removes the social learning opportunity and may deepen exclusion.

items.prompt: A single-case graph shows lower aggression immediately after a support begins, but baseline data were highly variable and aggression rises again near the end of intervention. What is the most defensible conclusion?
choices: The support is proven effective because the first intervention point is below the final baseline point.
The support failed because any later increase cancels all earlier improvement.
The student's behavior is random, so additional systematic observation cannot clarify the pattern.
The graph suggests a possible effect, but the team should examine level, trend, variability, fidelity, and replication before making a strong causal claim.
items.rationale: Visual analysis considers the whole pattern, not a single favorable or unfavorable point. Baseline variability, later overlap or trend, implementation fidelity, and replicated change affect confidence that the intervention—not another event—produced improvement.
choiceRationales: One point comparison ignores variability, trend, overlap, and alternative explanations.
A later increase matters but does not erase all information in the series.
Variability calls for careful design and measurement rather than abandoning inquiry.
This appropriately limits the claim while identifying evidence needed for stronger inference.

items.prompt: A student preparing for a community internship worries that corrective feedback will trigger walking off the job site. Which transition instruction is most likely to support durable employment success?
choices: Practice identifying early cues, requesting a brief approved pause or clarification, receiving simulated supervisor feedback, and returning to the task with the vocational partner using the same routine.
Ask the job coach to prevent supervisors from ever giving the student corrective feedback.
Teach the student to leave the site without notifying anyone whenever stress begins.
Focus transition instruction only on completing the technical job tasks because regulation is a school concern.
items.rationale: Rehearsing self-awareness, appropriate self-advocacy, feedback tolerance, and task reentry under conditions that resemble the internship promotes generalization. Coordination with the vocational partner makes the response feasible at the actual work site.
choiceRationales: It integrates regulation and communication with authentic workplace expectations and partners.
Eliminating all feedback is unrealistic and prevents learning a central employment skill.
Unannounced departure creates safety and employment risks rather than self-determination.
Work success depends on behavioral and self-advocacy skills as well as technical performance.

items.prompt: A teacher sees a student's public social-media post that includes a specific threat to harm a named classmate the next school day. What is the most appropriate professional response?
choices: Reply publicly to question the student and ask other users whether the threat is serious.

Follow the school's threat and safety reporting procedures immediately, preserve relevant information as permitted, notify designated personnel, and avoid conducting an independent online investigation.
Ignore the post because it appeared outside school hours and on the student's personal account.
Share a screenshot in the staff group chat so every employee can decide how to respond.
items.rationale: A specific prospective threat warrants prompt action through established safety channels regardless of where it was noticed. Designated personnel can assess and respond while the teacher avoids public engagement, rumor, excessive disclosure, or an untrained investigation.
choiceRationales: Public questioning can escalate the situation, compromise privacy, and interfere with assessment.
This activates trained, documented safety processes while limiting the teacher's role appropriately.
Off-campus origin does not justify ignoring information about a credible school safety concern.
Broad informal sharing exceeds need-to-know communication and can spread sensitive information.

items.prompt: When evaluating the validity and accessibility of behavioral or academic evidence, a student who uses a communication device is evaluated through spoken responses only. What should be changed?
choices: Translate the final score but keep the inaccessible administration.
Use the native language or appropriate communication mode and a format most likely to show what the learner knows, unless clearly infeasible.
Assume low performance proves disability.
Exclude parent and language-history information.
items.rationale: Evaluation procedures must reduce racial, cultural, linguistic, sensory, manual, and communication barriers so results reflect the intended aptitude or achievement rather than an access limitation. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

"Translate the final score but keep the inaccessible administration." misses the observable definition, prevention, function, replacement skill, dignity, evidence, safety, or legal requirement. Evaluation procedures must reduce racial, cultural, linguistic, sensory, manual, and communication barriers so results reflect the intended aptitude or achievement rather than an access limitation. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

Correct. Evaluation procedures must reduce racial, cultural, linguistic, sensory, manual, and communication barriers so results reflect the intended aptitude or achievement rather than an access limitation. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

Not the best answer. "Assume low performance proves disability." misses the observable definition, prevention, function, replacement skill, dignity, evidence, safety, or legal requirement. Evaluation procedures must reduce racial, cultural, linguistic, sensory, manual, and communication barriers so results reflect the intended aptitude or achievement rather than an access limitation. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

Not the best answer. "Exclude parent and language-history information." misses the observable definition, prevention, function, replacement skill, dignity, evidence, safety, or legal requirement. items.prompt: When evaluating the validity and accessibility of behavioral or academic evidence, a teacher wants immediate evidence about which step in a strategy students misunderstand. What should be used?
choices: A distant annual accountability score only.
An intelligence test unrelated to the lesson.
A brief formative task or observation aligned to the taught steps, followed by error analysis.
A final course grade after instruction ends.
items.rationale: Formative assessment supplies timely, instructionally relevant evidence during learning. Alignment and error analysis help the teacher decide what to reteach, model differently, or release to independent practice. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.

"A distant annual accountability score only." misses the observable definition, prevention, function, replacement skill, dignity, evidence, safety, or legal requirement. items.prompt: When evaluating the validity and accessibility of behavioral or academic evidence, a student understands science content but cannot manipulate the standard answer sheet. How should the assessment be administered?
choices: Lower the content standard automatically.
Score unanswered items as lack of science knowledge.
Give hints that reveal the correct content answers.
Provide an appropriate response accommodation that removes the motor barrier without changing the content construct.
items.rationale: An appropriate accommodation improves access while preserving the construct. The team should avoid both construct-irrelevant motor barriers and supports that supply the knowledge being measured. For 5372 preparation, define behavior observably, examine ecological and instructional context, distinguish correlation from function, teach a usable replacement skill, use the least intensive effective response, preserve dignity and belonging, and monitor both outcomes and implementation.
choiceRationales: Not the best answer. items.prompt: A learner is working through this task: “A teacher asks the behavior specialist to send a student's detailed incident log to every staff member in the building ‘so everyone knows what to expect.’ What is the most appropriate response?” A candidate selects “Send the full log because all school employees automatically need access to every student's behavior information”. Which feedback most directly identifies the problem with that selection?
choices: This limits disclosure to role-relevant, implementation-focused information through proper channels.
Employment at the school does not by itself establish a need for the complete record.
Confidentiality permits appropriate sharing with personnel who need information to serve the student.
Indirect identification can still disclose protected information and is not a valid safeguard.
items.rationale: The candidate selected “Send the full log because all school employees automatically need access to every student's behavior information”. Employment at the school does not by itself establish a need for the complete record. This feedback directly evaluates the selected response. “Identify staff with a legitimate educational role, share only the information needed to implement safety and instruction, and use objective language through approved channels” remains the source-supported response. Effective collaboration must be balanced with privacy: relevant staff need actionable information for their educational responsibilities, not unrestricted access to a detailed history. Minimum-necessary, objective communication also reduces stigma and rumor.
